effect_mechanism: Agroforestry diversifies farm income through timber, fruit, fodder, and non-timber forest products, and can provide direct dietary benefits from edible trees. Income diversification buffers against crop failure events and reduces seasonal food insecurity. Long-term carbon revenue streams (where accessible) can provide additional cash income to smallholders.
conditionality: Poverty reduction effect is conditional on functional market access for agroforestry products and sufficient farm size to absorb tree integration without reducing food crop area below subsistence thresholds. Tenure security required for smallholders to invest in long-rotation tree crops.

economic_value_range.source_note: Wide range reflects variation from subsistence parkland systems (~$50/ha/yr in additional income) to intensively managed silvoarable or carbon-credited systems (~$500/ha/yr). Marinis et al. (2022) report NPV of CAD $1.186 billion at landscape scale. Palma et al. (2007) demonstrate positive iNPV for European silvoarable systems.

justification: Ahmad & Goparaju (2017) position agroforestry explicitly as a livelihood improvement and poverty reduction strategy in Jharkhand, India. Brandt et al. (2015) identify agroforestry as a CSA practice that directly targets food security and income stability in Kenya's poorest counties. [archetype: Long horizon archetype: 5–15 years to positive net cashflow; patient capital, carbon finance, or output-based aid required during establishment phase. CrossBoundary classification: long_horizon.]

effect_mechanism: Trees in agroforestry systems sequester carbon in aboveground biomass (stem, branches, leaves) and belowground (roots). Agroforestry also improves soil organic carbon through litter inputs, root turnover, and reduced tillage frequency. Compared to open cropland, agroforestry systems store 2–5× more carbon per hectare in mature states.
conditionality: Carbon sequestration rates vary widely by species, climate, soil, and management. High rates apply only after canopy closure (typically 5–10 years post-establishment). Permanence requires long-term tree retention and management.

economic_value_range.source_note: Based on sequestration rates of 1–10 t CO₂/ha/yr × carbon price of $10–30/t. Kiziridis et al. (2026) map silvopastoral carbon sequestration potential for climate policy. Mendonça et al. (2023) use SOC sequestration potential as the primary prioritisation criterion.

justification: Kiziridis et al. (2026) explicitly optimise agroforestry siting to maximise carbon sequestration in Spain and Greece. Mendonça et al. (2023) use SOC sequestration as the leading environmental criterion for prioritising agroforestry areas in Brazil. Palma et al. (2007) demonstrate carbon sequestration as a primary environmental performance metric for European silvoarable systems. [archetype: Carbon revenue streams begin only after MRV verification (typically 3–5 years post-establishment). Carbon finance requires upfront technical assistance costs of $50–200/ha. Most viable for large-scale aggregated schemes.]

effect_mechanism: Agroforestry diversifies farm output to include fruits, nuts, fodder, and fuelwood alongside staple crops, reducing household food insecurity. Tree shade moderates heat stress on crops, and improved soil organic matter from leaf litter increases long-term soil fertility and yield stability. In drought years, deep-rooted trees can maintain partial productivity while annual crops fail.
conditionality: Food security benefit requires that tree integration does not compete excessively with food crops for light and water, especially during crop establishment. Compatible species pairing and appropriate tree density are critical. Effect may be negative in the short term (1–3 years) if tree establishment reduces crop area.
justification: Brandt et al. (2015) identify food security improvement as the primary CSA outcome targeted by agroforestry in East Africa. Ahmad et al. (2020) frame the South Asia agroforestry scale-up explicitly around food security and climate resilience co-benefits. [v0.3.0 reroute: food_security_risk → production_gap per T5 ratification (issue #34); see methodology/opportunity_space_T5.md.]

effect_mechanism: Agroforestry introduces structural complexity and functional diversity into agricultural landscapes, creating habitat for invertebrates, birds, and small mammals. Tree species diversity in agroforestry systems supports pollinator communities and pest-control predators. Riparian and corridor agroforestry enables connectivity between fragmented natural habitat patches.
conditionality: Biodiversity benefit requires diverse tree species selection (not monoculture timber plantations). Greatest benefit in landscapes highly degraded by intensive monoculture agriculture with little remaining natural habitat. Effect may be limited near intact natural forests where conversion to agroforestry could reduce habitat quality.
justification: Pinto et al. (2019) demonstrate agroforestry (Montado) as the primary biodiversity conservation vehicle in Portugal's agricultural landscapes, using spatial prioritisation to identify high-value agroforestry zones. Chuma et al. (2021) site agroforestry in the buffer zone of the Itombwe Reserve to support wildlife habitat connectivity. Nath et al. (2021) include biodiversity indicators in the Eastern Himalayan agroforestry suitability framework.

effect_mechanism: Tree root systems bind soil and increase macroporosity, reducing surface runoff and water erosion. Leaf litter and organic matter inputs increase soil organic carbon, improving soil structure, water retention, and fertility. Windbreak and shelterbelt agroforestry systems reduce wind erosion. Nitrogen-fixing trees (Leucaena, Faidherbia, Gliricidia) improve soil nitrogen status, reducing fertiliser dependency.
conditionality: Soil protection effect requires established canopy and functioning root systems (typically 3–7 years post-establishment). Effect may be delayed or limited in very degraded, compacted, or saline soils that require additional soil rehabilitation inputs.

economic_value_range.source_note: Avoided fertiliser costs and soil remediation costs. Castle et al. (2025) use soil erosion reduction (GloSEM model) as a primary agroforestry co-benefit. Palma et al. (2007) include nitrate leaching reduction and erosion control in European silvoarable economic assessment.

justification: Castle et al. (2025) prioritise agroforestry in areas with high water and wind erosion risk as the primary targeting criterion. Palma et al. (2007) demonstrate significant reduction in soil erosion (RUSLE) and nitrate leaching as environmental performance metrics. Mendonça et al. (2022, 2023) include soil organic carbon and slope stability as priority criteria. [archetype: Soil improvement benefits compound over time; economic value realised through reduced input costs and sustained yield maintenance over decades.]

effect_mechanism: Agroforestry tree canopy reduces evapotranspiration from crop and soil surfaces, lowers ambient temperature by 2–5°C under canopy, and hydraulic lift by deep-rooted trees moves subsoil water to upper layers. Combined effects maintain soil moisture during dry spells and reduce crop heat stress. Parkland agroforestry in semi-arid Sahel shows 20–80% higher crop yields under drought conditions compared to open fields.
conditionality: Drought mitigation effect strongest in semi-arid to sub-humid contexts (AI 0.3–0.65). In humid zones, drought is less limiting so the marginal benefit is smaller. Effect requires established trees (3–7 years establishment).
justification: Brandt et al. (2015) demonstrate agroforestry as the top-ranked CSA practice for drought mitigation in East Africa's dryland farming systems. Mendonça et al. (2022) map agroforestry as a high-priority drought adaptation measure in Brazil. Ahmad et al. (2020) quantify land potential for scaling agroforestry in South Asia specifically in drought-exposed rainfed systems.

effect_mechanism: Agroforestry establishment requires upfront investment in planting material (seedlings), labour for planting, establishment maintenance, and often fencing or protection from livestock browsing. Costs are higher for intensively managed silvoarable systems compared to low-input parkland or FMNR systems.
conditionality: Establishment cost is highly variable: FMNR and farmer-managed regeneration systems have near-zero direct costs. High-value commercial silvoarable systems (walnut, oak, fruit trees) have establishment costs of $500–1500/ha. Subsidies, carbon finance, or output-based aid can offset establishment costs.

economic_value_range.source_note: Low end: FMNR systems with minimal planting material cost. High end: intensively managed commercial silvoarable systems in Europe (Palma et al. 2007). Marinis et al. (2022) report detailed NPV calculations including silviculture costs for DR Congo context.

justification: Baldwin et al. (2022) build an explicit habitat creation cost surface to optimise agroforestry siting under budget constraints. Unger & Lakes (2023) quantify implementation cost savings between natural regeneration and active tree planting. Marinis et al. (2022) calculate full NPV including silviculture costs. [archetype: CrossBoundary long_horizon archetype: large upfront establishment costs with returns deferred 5–15 years. Requires patient capital, output-based aid, or advance carbon credit payments to bridge establishment financing gap.]

effect_mechanism: Proximity to road networks and markets enables farmers to access seedlings, technical inputs, and extension services for agroforestry establishment, and to sell timber, fruit, and non-timber forest products at commercially viable prices. Market access is a key determinant of whether agroforestry investment generates returns sufficient to justify the long payback period.
conditionality: Market access effect is strongest for commercial agroforestry sub-types (timber, fruits). Subsistence-oriented parkland agroforestry is less sensitive to road access. Effect is strongly context-dependent: carbon credit access is less dependent on road proximity than timber markets.
justification: Chuma et al. (2021) include road access distance as an explicit suitability criterion, recognising market access as a key system constraint. Castle et al. (2025) include infrastructure connectivity as a social-ecological suitability layer. Brandt et al. (2015) identify market linkages as a key enabling condition for CSA/agroforestry uptake in East Africa. [archetype: Market access is a prerequisite for commercial viability of long-horizon investments. Without market linkages, agroforestry remains subsistence-scale and carbon credit participation is unlikely.]
